skill.name: improve-codebase-architecture
skill.description: Find architecture refactoring opportunities by reviewing domain context, ADRs, module depth, interfaces, seams, adapters, and testability constraints.
skill.summary: Identify deepening opportunities that make a codebase more testable, local, and easier for AI agents to navigate.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The static high-risk weak-cryptography and shell-execution hits are false positives from prose, markdown file names, links, and inline backticks. No malicious intent, prompt injection, network exfiltration, real command execution, or executable cryptography was found. The skill has a low filesystem risk because it instructs assistants to read project documentation and may update CONTEXT.md or ADR files.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Reads and Documentation Updates
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to read CONTEXT.md and ADR files, and later update CONTEXT.md or offer ADR creation when architectural decisions crystallize. This is legitimate for an architecture-review skill, but users should expect repository documentation changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly instruct reading project documentation and updating CONTEXT.md or ADR documentation. The behavior is transparent and scoped to repository documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts are caused by markdown prose, architecture vocabulary, file names ending in .md, and words such as design or interface. No cryptographic operation or weak algorithm usage is present in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain markdown guidance only, with no executable code paths or cryptographic API calls. The matched strings are normal documentation text.

low_findings.title: False Positive Shell Backtick Matches
low_findings.description: The shell-execution alerts are markdown inline-code backticks around file names, terminology, and examples. They are documentation formatting, not Ruby or shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched backticks appear in markdown prose and file references. There is no script block, shell command, interpreter invocation, or user-input command construction.

low_findings.title: False Positive Path Traversal and Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: Relative links to sibling skill documentation and instructions to explore a codebase are expected for this repository-analysis skill. No evidence found of unauthorized filesystem traversal, secret collection, or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths are markdown documentation links, and the exploration instruction is scoped to architecture review. The content does not ask for sensitive files, credentials, or system inventory.

content.value_statement: Large codebases often accumulate shallow modules, unclear interfaces, and tests that lock in implementation details. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a structured architecture review focused on deeper modules and better seams.
actual_capabilities: Reads project context documents and ADRs before proposing architecture changes.
Identifies shallow modules where interfaces expose too much implementation complexity.
Presents numbered deepening opportunities with files, problems, solutions, and benefits.
Guides follow-up design conversations around constraints, dependencies, seams, and tests.
Compares alternative interface designs using module depth, locality, leverage, and adapter strategy.
May update CONTEXT.md or propose ADR entries when architectural decisions need documentation.
limitations: It does not automatically implement refactors unless the user asks for implementation work.
Its advice depends on the quality of existing context documents, ADRs, and code structure.
It may need human judgment when architecture trade-offs conflict with business constraints.
It is not intended for simple formatting, linting, or one-file cleanup tasks.

use_cases.title: Review a growing service
use_cases.description: Find modules where business rules have spread across callers and propose deeper interfaces that concentrate behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare a refactoring plan
use_cases.description: Create a prioritized list of architecture changes before a team invests in implementation work.

use_cases.title: Improve tests around unstable code
use_cases.description: Identify seams and adapters that let tests verify behavior through stable interfaces instead of internals.

prompt_templates.title: Find Architecture Friction
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an unfamiliar repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review the repository architecture. Read CONTEXT.md and ADRs first, then list deepening opportunities with files, problems, solutions, and testability benefits.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Problem Area
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review of one module cluster
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill on the payment and order modules. Identify shallow modules, coupling across seams, and places where a deeper interface would improve locality.

prompt_templates.title: Explore a Candidate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the grilling loop for candidate 2. Walk through constraints, dependencies, seam placement, adapter choices, and which tests should survive the refactor.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Interface Designs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the interface design process for the selected candidate. Generate several different interface options, compare them by depth and locality, then recommend one design.

output_examples.input: Review this repository for architecture improvements.
output: A numbered list of deepening opportunities.
Each item names the affected files, current friction, proposed change, and testing benefit.
The assistant asks which candidate should be explored next.

output_examples.input: Explore the order intake candidate in more depth.
output: A design discussion covering constraints, dependencies, seam placement, and adapter needs.
A clearer module shape expressed in the project's domain vocabulary.
A recommendation for which tests should move to the new interface.

output_examples.input: Compare possible interfaces for this refactor.
output: Several interface options with different design priorities.
A prose comparison based on leverage, locality, and trade-offs.

best_practices: Start with a clean working tree so proposed documentation updates are easy to review.
Provide domain context and ADRs before asking for architecture recommendations.
Choose one candidate at a time for deeper design work before implementation.
anti_patterns: Using the skill for broad rewrites without first selecting a specific deepening candidate.
Asking it to create interfaces before it has reviewed existing context and constraints.
Ignoring ADR conflicts when the proposed change contradicts a recorded decision.

faq.question: Does this skill change code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on analysis and design unless the user separately asks for implementation.

faq.question: Why does it read CONTEXT.md and ADR files?
faq.answer: Those files provide domain language and architectural decisions that should shape recommendations.

faq.question: Can it work without existing architecture documents?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill says to proceed silently if those files are absent.

faq.question: What does deepening mean here?
faq.answer: It means moving useful behavior behind a smaller interface so callers gain leverage and maintainers gain locality.

faq.question: Is this useful for tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It looks for interfaces that let tests verify behavior without depending on implementation details.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
